extracted_rules.resource: Right to exercise authority in marriage
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1] Again, as between the sexes, the male is by nature superior and the female inferior, the male ruler and the female subject. And the same must also necessarily apply in the case of mankind as a whole; therefore all men that differ as widely as the soul does from the body and the human being from the lower animal... these are by nature slaves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the male-female relationship as a natural hierarchy where the male is the continuous ruler and the female is the subject, meaning women lack the resource of equal authority in the domestic sphere.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1] These considerations therefore make clear the nature of the slave and his essential quality: one who is a human being belonging by nature not to himself but to another is by nature a slave, and a person is a human being belonging to another if being a man he is an article of property, and an article of property is an instrument for action separable from its owner.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly defines slaves as living tools or property that belongs to another, thereby lacking the resource of self-governance or self-ownership.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1] Hence the saying of the poets— ’Tis meet that Greeks should rule barbarians,— implying that barbarian and slave are the same in nature. . . . Hence even the art of war will by nature be in a manner an art of acquisition . . . that is properly employed both against wild animals and against such of mankind as though designed by nature for subjection refuse to submit to it, inasmuch as this warfare is by nature just.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle equates barbarians with natural slaves, arguing they are naturally intended for subjection by Greeks, which deprives them of the right to political independence.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1] For the male is by nature better fitted to command than the female... and the older and fully developed person than the younger and immature. . . . The rule of the father over the children on the other hand is that of a king; for the male parent is the ruler in virtue both of affection and of seniority, which is characteristic of royal government.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Children are categorized as immature and thus subject to the monarchical authority of the father, lacking the status of 'free and equal' granted to adult male citizens.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1] For the slave has not got the deliberative part at all, and the female has it, but without full authority, while the child has it, but in an undeveloped form. Hence the ruler must possess intellectual virtue in completeness . . . while each of the other parties must have that share of this virtue which is appropriate to them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that slaves entirely lack the deliberative capacity of the soul, which serves as the justification for their exclusion from political rights and self-direction.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1] For the slave has not got the deliberative part at all, and the female has it, but without full authority, while the child has it, but in an undeveloped form.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While women are noted to have a deliberative faculty (unlike slaves), it is described as being 'without authority', effectively excluding them from final decision-making power in the state or household.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1] For the mechanic artisan is under a sort of limited slavery, and whereas the slave is one of the natural classes, no shoemaker or other craftsman belongs to his trade by nature. . . . they frequently fall short in their tasks owing to intemperance.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Artisans are described as being in a state of limited slavery; while not slaves by nature, their occupation is seen as incompatible with the full virtue of a citizen, limiting their access to the 'good life'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§2] ...he that can foresee with his mind is naturally ruler and naturally master, and he that can do these things with his body is subject and naturally a slave; so that master and slave have the same interest. [...] [§6] These considerations therefore make clear the nature of the slave and his essential quality: one who is a human being belonging by nature not to himself but to another is by nature a slave.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines the slave as an 'article of property' who belongs entirely to a master, thereby lacking the resource of self-ownership based on the criterion of freedom.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§3] ...for every household is under the royal rule of its eldest member, so that the colonies from the household were so too, because of the kinship of their members. And this is what Homer means: And each one giveth law To sons and eke to spouses.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the household as a structure where the male head (husband/father) exercises rule over the wife, indicating women have less authority within the domestic partnership.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§3] Hence the saying of the poets— ’Tis meet that Greeks should rule barbarians,— implying that barbarian and slave are the same in nature.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author cites the prevailing Greek sentiment and poetic convention that justifies the subordination of non-Greeks (barbarians) to Greeks, equating their natural status to that of slaves.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to legal and personal autonomy
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§3] ...for every household is under the royal rule of its eldest member... And this is what Homer means: And each one giveth law To sons and eke to spouses.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes the 'royal rule' of the eldest member (the father) over his sons, meaning younger members of the lineage lack the resource of personal autonomy.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection of law and social partnership
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§4] ...a man that is by nature and not merely by fortune citiless is either low in the scale of humanity or above it (like the clanless, lawless, hearthless man reviled by Homer, for one by nature unsocial is also a lover of war) inasmuch as he is solitary, like an isolated piece at draughts.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle characterizes those without a city-state (citizenship) as lacking the protection of law and the benefits of social partnership, effectively placing them outside the human communal framework.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§6] Now the tools mentioned are instruments of production, whereas an article of property is an instrument of action... life is doing things, not making things; hence the slave is an assistant in the class of instruments of action.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes between tools that 'make' (production) and those that 'do' (action), categorizing the slave strictly as an instrument of action for the owner's use rather than a participant in independent production.

verbatim: [§7] Again, as between the sexes, the male is by nature superior and the female inferior, the male ruler and the female subject.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the hierarchical relationship between the sexes as a natural rule of governance where the female is systematically subjected to the male.

verbatim: [§7] The intention of nature therefore is to make the bodies also of freemen and of slaves different—the latter strong for necessary service, the former erect and unserviceable for such occupations, but serviceable for a life of citizenship.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that the purpose and bodily capacity of slaves is for manual labor, explicitly contrasting them with free men who have access to the 'life of citizenship'.

verbatim: [§8] for there is also such a thing as a slave or a man that is in slavery by law, for the law is a sort of agreement under which the things conquered in war are said to belong to their conquerors.
[§8] Therefore they do not mean to assert that Greeks themselves if taken prisoners are slaves, but that barbarians are.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a legal convention where non-Greeks (barbarians) captured in war are treated as property (slaves), a rule from which Greeks seek to exempt themselves.

verbatim: [§8] our nobles consider themselves noble not only in their own country but everywhere, but they think that barbarian noblemen are only noble in their own country—which implies that there are two kinds of nobility and of freedom, one absolute and the other relative.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explains a convention where the status of 'noble' is viewed as absolute for Greeks but only contextual and valid within their own borders for non-Greeks.

verbatim: [§9] Therefore all people rich enough to be able to avoid personal trouble have a steward who takes this office, while they themselves engage in politics or philosophy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a threshold of wealth that allows a group to delegate the management of staff and labor to a steward, providing them with the resource of leisure for politics or philosophy.

verbatim: [§11] Hence even the art of war will by nature be in a manner an art of acquisition (for the art of hunting is a part of it) that is properly employed both against wild animals and against such of mankind as though designed by nature for subjection refuse to submit to it, inasmuch as this warfare is by nature just.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the contemporary philosophical and legal justification for 'just war' as a means of acquiring property, specifically targeting those he categorizes as naturally intended for subjection (slaves) but who resist.

verbatim: [§11] One kind of acquisition therefore in the order of nature is a part of the household art, in accordance with which either there must be forthcoming or else that art must procure to be forthcoming a supply of those goods, capable of accumulation, which are necessary for life and useful for the community of city or household.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a specific, natural right for those managing a household to acquire and accumulate the material resources required for the survival and utility of the family unit.

verbatim: [§12] ...according to their needs for which they were forced to make their interchanges by way of barter, as also many barbarian tribes do still; for such tribes do not go beyond exchanging actual commodities for actual commodities, for example giving and taking wine for corn, and so with the various other things of the sort.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies 'barbarians' as a group that, in his view/time, lacks the resource of monetary systems and is restricted to the simpler, 'natural' method of commodity barter.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to fruits of the soil
verbatim: [§14] Hence the business of drawing provision from the fruits of the soil and from animals is natural to all. But, as we said, this art is twofold, one branch being of the nature of trade while the other belongs to the household art; and the latter branch is necessary and in good esteem
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that the extraction of resources from the environment for household use is a recognized and esteemed right of the householder, contrasting it with commercial trade.

verbatim: [§17] It is true that in most cases of republican government the ruler and the ruled interchange in turn (for they tend to be on in equal level in their nature and to have no difference at all) [...] but the male stands in this relationship to the female continuously.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes the political rule between equals (which rotates) from the domestic rule over women, which he describes as a permanent state of being ruled based on gender.

verbatim: [§17] but over the wife to exercise republican government and over the children monarchical [...] for the male parent is the ruler in virtue both of affection and of seniority, which is characteristic of royal government.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that children are subject to a monarchical form of rule by their father due to their lack of maturity and seniority, unlike the republican (equal) relation theoretically afforded to the wife.

verbatim: [§18] and that the temperance of a woman and that of a man are not the same, nor their courage and justice, [...] but the one is the courage of command, and the other that of subordination, and the case is similar with the other virtues.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that virtue is gender-specific; women's courage is defined specifically as the courage of 'subordination' rather than the 'command' afforded to men.

verbatim: [§18] For the free rules the slave, the male the female, and the man the child in a different way. And all possess the various parts of the soul, but possess them in different ways; for the slave has not got the deliberative part at all.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author defines the slave as inherently lacking the 'deliberative' capacity of the soul, which justifies their total exclusion from self-governance or higher social functions.

verbatim: [§18] and the female has it [the deliberative part], but without full authority, while the child has it, but in an undeveloped form.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While women are granted the capacity to deliberate (unlike slaves), the rule states this capacity lacks 'authority', placing them in a permanent state of being ruled by men.

verbatim: [§18] Or is their case entirely different? For the slave is a partner in his master’s life, but the artisan is more remote, and only so much of virtue falls to his share as of slavery— [§19] for the mechanic artisan is under a sort of limited slavery.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle categorizes the mechanic artisan as being in a state of 'limited slavery', suggesting their occupation prevents them from attaining the full virtue of a free citizen.

verbatim: [§19] it is necessary that the education both of the children and of the women should be carried on with a regard to the form of the constitution, if it makes any difference as regards the goodness of the state for the children and the women to be good.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule that the education of the group 'children' must be aligned with the state's constitution because they are the future 'partners' in government.

verbatim: [§20] among peoples where it is impossible because all the citizens are equal in their nature, yet at the same time it is only just, whether governing is a good thing or a bad, that all should partake in it, and for equals thus to submit to authority in turn imitates their being originally dissimilar; for some govern and others are governed by turn, as though becoming other persons.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the standard practice and principle in Greek city-states where citizens are considered natural equals, asserting their right to take turns in ruling and being ruled.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to gymnastic exercises and arms
verbatim: [§20] These [the Cretans] have conceded to their slaves all the same rights as they have themselves except that they are forbidden gymnastic exercises and the possession of arms.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly names the specific exclusions (gymnastics and arms) applied to the slave group in Crete compared to the free citizens.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit and own land
verbatim: [§20] And also nearly two-fifths of the whole area of the country is owned by women, because of the number of women who inherit estates and the practice of giving large dowries.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies a specific trend in Spartan society where the law allows women to accumulate a significant portion of land through inheritance and dowries.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from military service and taxes
verbatim: [§20] for they have a law releasing the man who has been father of three sons from military service, and exempting the father of four from all taxes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule links the status of fatherhood (an age-related life stage) to specific material and civic exemptions to encourage population growth.

verbatim: [§20] at Sparta each citizen pays a fixed poll-tax, failing which he is prevented by law from taking part in the government... for it is not easy for the very poor to participate, yet their ancestral regulation of the citizenship is that it is not to belong to one who is unable to pay this tax.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle points out that Spartan citizenship is contingent on the ability to pay for common meals, effectively stripping the poor (those lacking wealth) of their rights.

extracted_rules.resource: Eligibility for the office of Cosmos
verbatim: [§20] in Crete they do not elect the Cosmi from all the citizens but from certain clans, and the Elders from those who have held the office of Cosmos.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Access to high office in Crete is restricted based on lineage (specific clans) rather than general citizenship.

verbatim: [§20] they think that the rulers should be chosen not only for their merit but also for their wealth... it is a bad thing that the greatest offices of state, the kingship and the generalship, should be for sale.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In Carthage, wealth is a primary criterion for holding the highest offices, often involving the literal purchase of the position.

verbatim: [§20] whereas he [Solon] appointed all the offices from the notable and the wealthy, the Five-hundred-bushel class and the Teamsters and a third property-class called the Knighthood; while the fourth class, the Thetes, were admitted to no office.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes that under Solon's laws, the lowest wealth class (Thetes) was explicitly barred from holding administrative offices.

verbatim: [§20] a special law of his [Pittacus] is that if men commit any offence when drunk, they are to pay a larger fine than those who offend when sober.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The status of being in a specific biological state (drunkenness) is treated by Pittacus as a criterion for increased legal penalty.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to own the city in common
verbatim: [§21] To have nothing in common is clearly impossible for the state is essentially a form of community, and to begin with there is bound to be a common locality: a single city occupies a single site, and the single city belongs to its citizens in common.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies that in existing political systems, the defining nature of a city involves the citizens holding the physical site and the state in common.

verbatim: [§22] For even among the free and equal this principle must necessarily obtain, since all cannot govern at once: they must hold office for a year at a time or by some other arrangement or period; and in this manner it does actually come about that all govern
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the standard practice in Greek states where, because citizens are perceived as equals, the resource of political power is distributed through rotation.

verbatim: [§23] yet at the same time it is only just, whether governing is a good thing or a bad, that all should partake in it, and for equals thus to submit to authority in turn imitates their being originally dissimilar; for some govern and others are governed by turn, as though becoming other persons
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle observes that it is a matter of justice in current systems that equals share the resource of political authority through a system of turns.

verbatim: [§24] And it seems that this community of wives and sons is more serviceable for the Farmer class than for the Guardians; [§25] for there will be less friendship among them if their children and women are in common, and unfriendliness in the subject classes is a good thing with a view to their being submissive to authority and not making revolution.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that depriving the laboring/subject class of distinct family ties (a resource of social cohesion) is a strategic rule to ensure their submission.

verbatim: [§26] For instance in Sparta people use one another’s slaves as virtually their own, as well as horses and hounds, and also use the produce in the fields throughout the country if they need provisions on a journey.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a contemporary Spartan convention where citizens have the right to access the private resources (slaves, animals, crops) of other citizens for their own needs.

verbatim: [§28] These have conceded to their slaves all the same rights as they have themselves except that they are forbidden gymnastic exercises and the possession of arms.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific legal restriction in Crete where the group defined by the criterion of 'Freedom' (slaves) is denied access to physical training and weaponry.

verbatim: [§28] These have conceded to their slaves all the same rights as they have themselves except that they are forbidden gymnastic exercises and the possession of arms.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the Cretan context provided, slaves are explicitly restricted from owning or carrying weapons, which is a key resource for self-defense and status.

verbatim: [§28] So that in the upshot no other regulation will have been enacted except the exemption of the Guardians from the work of agriculture, which is a measure that even now the Spartans attempt to introduce.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes a specific regulation (attempted or practiced) that releases the warrior class (Guardians/Spartans) from the burden of manual farming labor.

verbatim: [§32] ...but the compulsion put upon the richer citizens to attend the assembly and vote for magistrates or perform any other political function, while the others are allowed to do as they like, is oligarchical...
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a specific legal mechanism in the 'Laws' (which he treats as reflecting actual oligarchical tendencies) where the wealthy are legally forced to participate in the assembly, whereas the poor are not.

verbatim: [§32] ...as is the endeavor to secure that a majority of the magistrates shall be drawn from the wealthy and that the highest offices shall be filled from the highest of the classes assessed by wealth.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a rule where access to the most important political resources (high magistracies) is restricted to those belonging to the highest property assessment classes.

verbatim: [§33] ...there is legislation to prevent the sale of estates, as at Locri there is a law that a man shall not sell unless he can prove that manifest misfortune has befallen him and also there is legislation to preserve the old allotments...
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle cites the laws of Locri as a real-world example where landowners are prohibited from selling their property except under extreme, proven distress, limiting their right to dispose of assets.

verbatim: [§33] ...other states have a law prohibiting the acquisition of land to any amount that the individual may desire...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes that certain states impose a ceiling on the amount of land a citizen can own, restricting the resource of property acquisition based on existing wealth.

verbatim: [§32] ...except that it was not to be compulsory for all to vote for those to be elected from the members of the third or the fourth class... some of the lower orders will abstain from voting as it is not compulsory.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle contrasts the requirements of different wealth classes, noting that lower property classes (3rd and 4th) are granted the resource of choice/abstention in certain elections where the wealthy are compelled.

verbatim: [§33] ...a levelling would most easily be effected by the rich giving dowries but not receiving them and the poor receiving but not giving them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Phaleas proposed a rule where the wealthy must pay out dowries but are forbidden from receiving them, transferring material resources to the poor to achieve economic equality.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§35] And it is clear from Phaleas’s legislation that he makes the citizen-population a small one, inasmuch as all the artisans are to be publicly owned slaves and are not to furnish any complement of the citizen-body.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of evaluating the constitution of Phaleas, Aristotle notes a rule where those who practice crafts (artisans) are legally classified as public slaves and are specifically barred from the citizen body.

extracted_rules.group: Children of those who died in war
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§36] He proposed a law that ... the children of those who died in war should have their maintenance from the state, in the belief that this had never yet been provided by law among other people—but as a matter of fact this law exists at present both at Athens and in others of the cities.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle confirms that in Athens and other contemporary cities, the law provides the resource of state-funded maintenance to the specific group of children whose fathers died in military service.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§35] and also the baseness of human beings is a thing insatiable, and though at the first a dole of only two obols is enough, yet when this has now become an established custom, they always want more, until they get to an unlimited amount.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle references the contemporary practice of distributing 2-obol doles to the populace as an established custom, identifying a transfer of public funds to a specific group.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§35] He also proposed to establish one supreme court of justice, to which were to be carried up all the cases at law thought to have been decided wrongly, and this court he made to consist of certain selected elders.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the described constitution of Hippodamus, the specific right to serve on the highest court of appeal is restricted to the group defined by the criterion of age (elders).

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§36] The artisans, the farmers and the military class all participate in the government, though the farmers have not got arms and the artisans neither arms nor land, which makes them almost the slaves of those who possess the arms.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In analyzing the proposed system of Hippodamus, Aristotle points out that the occupational group of farmers is specifically denied the resource of possessing arms, creating a dependency on the military class.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§36] The artisans, the farmers and the military class all participate in the government, though the farmers have not got arms and the artisans neither arms nor land, which makes them almost the slaves of those who possess the arms.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that in the tri-partite division of the city, artisans do not have the right to land, distinguishing them from the farmers and the military class.

extracted_rules.rule: Warrior class access to public food
extracted_rules.resource: Right to subsistence from public land
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§35] He divided the land into three parts, one sacred, one public and one private: ... common land to provide the warrior class with food, and private land to be owned by the farmers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The provision defines a specific resource (food from common land) that is legally earmarked for the military class, distinguishing them from those who own private land.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§38] as for example at Cyme there is a law relating to trials for murder, that if the prosecutor on the charge of murder produces a certain number of his own relatives as witnesses, the defendant is guilty of the murder.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author cites a specific existing law in Cyme where the status of being a relative of the prosecutor grants the legal power to determine a defendant's guilt through testimony.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to leisure from menial occupations
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§38] Now it is a thing admitted that a state that is to be well governed must be provided with leisure from menial occupations; but how this is to be provided it is not easy to ascertain. The serf class in Thessaly repeatedly rose against its masters, and so did the Helots at Sparta.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that leisure is a resource reserved for the governing class, while the 'serf class' is defined by the performance of the menial occupations that provide it, excluding them from this resource.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§40] And also nearly two-fifths of the whole area of the country is owned by women, because of the number of women who inherit estates and the practice of giving large dowries; yet it would have been better if dowries had been prohibited by law or limited to a small or moderate amount.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a factual state of affairs in Sparta where women have gained significant access to land ownership through inheritance and dowries, despite his personal criticism of the practice.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§39] so that in all constitutions in which the position of the women is badly regulated one half of the state must be deemed to have been neglected in framing the law. And this has taken place in the state under consideration,for the lawgiver wishing the whole city to be of strong character displays his intention clearly in relation to the men, but in the case of the women has entirely neglected the matter.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author notes that the Spartan legislator deliberately left women outside the scope of the laws that governed the conduct and character of male citizens.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§41] for they have a law releasing the man who has been father of three sons from military service, and exempting the father of four from all taxes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This is an explicit legal rule in Sparta where a specific biological/parental status grants the resource of exemption from state duties.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§41] for they have a law releasing the man who has been father of three sons from military service, and exempting the father of four from all taxes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Similar to the military exemption, the law grants a material financial resource (tax exemption) based on meeting the criteria of fathering four sons.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§41] Moreover the regulations for the Ephorate are also bad. For this office has absolute control over their most important affairs, but the Ephors are appointed from the entire people, so that quite poor men often happen to get into the office.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the eligibility for the Ephorate as being open to 'the entire people' (meaning all citizens), regardless of wealth, which he contrasts with other more restrictive offices.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to alienate land by gift/bequest
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§40] for the lawgiver made it dishonorable to sell a family’s existing estate, and did so rightly, but he granted liberty to alienate land at will by gift or bequest.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal right granted to owners to transfer property as they wish, provided it is not a direct sale.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§42] For he intends the organization of the common tables to be democratic, but when regulated by the law in this manner it works out as by no means democratic; for it is not easy for the very poor to participate, yet their ancestral regulation of the citizenship is that it is not to belong to one who is unable to pay this tax.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a specific Spartan legal rule where maintaining citizenship is contingent upon the ability to pay a specific tax/contribution for the public mess-tables.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§44] for at Sparta each citizen pays a fixed poll-tax, failing which he is prevented by law from taking part in the government, as has been said before.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that failure to pay the required poll-tax results in the legal loss of the right to participate in the government.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§44] At Sparta, as the election is made from all the citizens, the common people sharing in the highest office desire the maintenance of the constitution, but in Crete they do not elect the Cosmi from all the citizens but from certain clans.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Eligibility for the high office of 'Cosmos' in Crete is restricted to members of specific lineages or clans rather than the general citizenry.

extracted_rules.resource: Eligibility for the Council of Elders
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§44] but in Crete they do not elect the Cosmi from all the citizens but from certain clans, and the Elders from those who have held the office of Cosmos.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule for the Cretan Council of Elders requires that a candidate must have previously held the office of Cosmos, making it an occupational or career-track requirement.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§46] But the Carthaginian system diverges from aristocracy in the direction of oligarchy most signally in respect of a certain idea that is shared by the mass of mankind; they think that the rulers should be chosen not only for their merit but also for their wealth, as it is not possible for a poor man to govern well or to have leisure for his duties.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly identifies wealth as a legal or customary criterion for holding high office in Carthage, justified by the need for leisure.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§46] And if it is necessary to look to the question of means for the sake of leisure, it is a bad thing that the greatest offices of state, the kingship and the generalship, should be for sale.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author notes that top offices in Carthage are 'for sale,' effectively creating a wealth-based entry barrier that gives the wealthy access to these specific resources.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§44] but in Crete the system is more communal, for out of all the crops and cattle produced from the public lands... one part is assigned... for the public mess-tables, so that all the citizens are maintained from the common funds, women and children as well as men.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Being a member of the citizen body (including their families) in Crete entitles one to material maintenance from the state's public resources.

verbatim: [§48] ...he does appear to have founded the democracy by constituting the jury-courts from all the citizens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes Solon’s historical layout of the Athenian state where the right to participate in the judicial process was extended to the entire citizen body, a fundamental shift toward democracy.

verbatim: [§48] ...whereas he appointed all the offices from the notable and the wealthy, the Five-hundred-bushel class and the Teamsters and a third property-class called the Knighthood; while the fourth class, the Thetes, were admitted to no office.

verbatim: [§185] For until puberty we should apply lighter exercises, forbidding hard diet and severe exertions, in order that nothing may hinder the growth; for there is no small proof that too severe training can produce this result in the fact that in the list of Olympic victors one would only find two or three persons who have won both as men and as boys.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text specifies an age-based rule where children are protected from the 'severe exertion' required of adult athletes to ensure proper growth.

verbatim: [§185] [...] it remains therefore that it is useful as a pastime in leisure, which is evidently the purpose for which people actually introduce it, for they rank it as a form of pastime that they think proper for free men.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Music is explicitly categorized as a resource appropriate for the leisure of free citizens, distinguishing it from merely 'useful' or 'menial' skills.

verbatim: [§185] But professional musicians we speak of as vulgar people, and indeed we think it not manly to perform music, except when drunk or for fun. [...] we do not consider performing to be proper for free men, but somewhat menial; and indeed performers do become vulgar.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a social rule where free men are protected/discouraged from professional performance, which is seen as degrading and suited only for 'vulgar' people.

verbatim: [§185] since the audience is of two classes, one freemen and educated people, and the other the vulgar class composed of mechanics and laborers and other such persons, the latter sort also must be assigned competitions and shows for relaxation.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a societal structure where entertainment and 'harmonies' are allocated differently based on the occupational/social class of the audience.

verbatim: [§185] for instance, those whose powers have waned through lapse of time cannot easily sing the highly strung harmonies, but to persons of that age nature suggests the relaxed harmonies. Hence even with a view to the period of life that is to follow, that of the comparatively old, it is proper to engage in the harmonies and melodies of this kind.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Older citizens are identified as a specific group for whom different, less strenuous musical standards are applied.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§186] And inasmuch as the end for the whole state is one, it is manifest that education also must necessarily be one and the same for all and that the superintendence of this must be public, and not on private lines, in the way in which at present each man superintends the education of his own children, teaching them privately, and whatever special branch of knowledge he thinks fit. But matters of public interest ought to be under public supervision; at the same time we ought not to think that any of the citizens belongs to himself, but that all belong to the state, for each is a part of the state.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that because citizens belong to the state, their education must be a public resource or responsibility rather than a private matter, establishing a rule for the citizen group.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§187] Hence we entitle vulgar all such arts as deteriorate the condition of the body, and also the industries that earn wages; for they make the mind preoccupied and degraded.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies those in wage-earning occupations as being subjected to conditions that degrade the mind, implying a loss of the resource of mental/intellectual integrity compared to 'liberal' groups.

extracted_rules.rule: Menial study exclusion for free men
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§187] It is therefore not difficult to see that the young must be taught those useful arts that are indispensably necessary; but it is clear that they should not be taught all the useful arts, those pursuits that are liberal being kept distinct from those that are illidberal, and that they must participate in such among the useful arts as will not render the person who participates in them vulgar. A task and also an art or a science must be deemed vulgar if it renders the body or soul or mind of free men useless for the employments and actions of virtue.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Free men are explicitly granted a specific type of resource (liberal education/arts) while being restricted from 'vulgar' or 'illiberal' arts to preserve their capacity for virtue.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§189] For until puberty we should apply lighter exercises, forbidding hard diet and severe exertions, in order that nothing may hinder the growth; [§190] for there is no small proof that too severe training can produce this result in the fact that in the list of Olympic victors one would only find two or three persons who have won both as men and as boys, because when people go into training in youth the severe exercises rob them of their strength.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines a specific legal or societal rule regarding the age of puberty, protecting younger boys from 'severe exertions' to ensure natural growth.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to music and leisure training
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§188] it remains therefore that it is useful as a pastime in leisure, which is evidently the purpose for which people actually introduce it, for they rank it as a form of pastime that they think proper for free men. ... It is clear therefore that there is a form of education in which boys should be trained not because it is useful or necessary but as being liberal and noble.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines music and leisure-based learning as a resource specifically reserved and deemed appropriate for 'free men' and their children, as opposed to slaves or manual laborers.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§193] Such considerations therefore prove that children should be trained in music so as actually to take part in its performance; and it is not difficult to distinguish what is suitable and unsuitable for various ages, and to refute those who assert that the practice of music is vulgar.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that during youth, individuals should learn through active performance to develop their judgment, transitioning to a purely appreciative role as they age.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§193] for the sake of judging them, it is therefore proper for the pupils when young actually to engage in the performances, though when they get older they should be released from performing, but be able to judge what is beautiful and enjoy it rightly
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule where the resource (exemption from the 'vulgar' labor of playing) is granted specifically to those who have matured past youth.

extracted_rules.rule: Exclusion of free men from professional music
extracted_rules.resource: Protection from menial or professional labor
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§195] and we count performance in competitions as professional, for the performer does not take part in it for his own improvement, but for his hearers’ pleasure, and that a vulgar pleasure, owing to which we do not consider performing to be proper for free men, but somewhat menial
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines professional performance as 'menial' and beneath the status of a free man, thereby creating a social boundary that protects citizens from certain occupations.

extracted_rules.resource: Reputation of manliness and civic virtue
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§191] But professional musicians we speak of as vulgar people, and indeed we think it not manly to perform music, except when drunk or for fun. [...] [§195] and indeed performers do become vulgar, since the object at which they aim is a low one
extracted_rules.reasoning: Professional musicians are systematically categorized as 'vulgar' and 'unmanly,' which functions as a loss of the resource of political/social standing.

extracted_rules.rule: Free youth restricted from flute playing
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§194] Flutes must not be introduced into education, nor any other professional instrument... Hence former ages rightly rejected its use by the young and the free, although at first they had employed it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author notes a societal restriction: while 'freemen' once played the flute, the practice was later rejected for the 'free' and 'young' because it was deemed non-intellectual and unsuited for education.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§194] and not merely the charm common to all music, which even some lower animals enjoy, as well as a multitude of slaves and children.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While excluded from high-level 'beautiful' musical education, slaves are identified as a group that has access to and enjoys the basic sensory pleasure of common music.

verbatim: [§197] And all agree that the Dorian mode is more sedate and of a specially manly character. Moreover since we praise and say that we ought to pursue the mean between extremes, and the Dorian mode has this nature in relation to the other harmonies, it is clear that it suits the younger pupils to be educated rather in the Dorian melodies.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle establishes a rule for the educational curriculum of the young based on the 'manly' and moderate character of the Dorian mode, contrasting it with harmonies suited for other ages.

verbatim: [§196] and since the audience is of two classes, one freemen and educated people, and the other the vulgar class composed of mechanics and laborers and other such persons, the latter sort also must be assigned competitions and shows for relaxation; and just as their souls are warped from the natural state, so those harmonies and melodies that are highly strung and irregular in coloration are deviations, but people of each sort receive pleasure from what is naturally suited to them
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a societal rule where specific types of music and theatrical shows are assigned to the working class (mechanics and laborers) because their souls are deemed 'warped' compared to freemen.

verbatim: [§197] for instance, those whose powers have waned through lapse of time cannot easily sing the highly strung harmonies, but to persons of that age nature suggests the relaxed harmonies. [...] Hence even with a view to the period of life that is to follow, that of the comparatively old, it is proper to engage in the harmonies and melodies of this kind too
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines a rule based on age where older individuals are permitted and encouraged to use 'relaxed' harmonies because they no longer possess the strength for highly strung ones.

verbatim: [§196] but use the most ethical ones for education, and the active and passionate kinds for listening to when others are performing [...] but for education, as has been said, the ethical class of melodies and of harmonies must be employed.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author distinguishes between the 'vulgar' and the 'educated', prescribing specific ethical harmonies as a resource for the education of the latter group.
